tip: Stay hydrated — drink water every 20 minutes while riding, even if you don't feel thirsty.
Shift gears BEFORE you reach a steep hill, not when you're already struggling up it.
Aim for a cadence of 70–90 RPM (pedal rotations per minute) — it protects your knees.
Always wear your helmet before starting any ride. No ride is worth risking your head.
Clean and lube your chain every 100–150 km to keep your drivetrain running smoothly.
Ride in the early morning (5–7 AM) in the Philippines to beat the heat and traffic.
Never use cross-chain gears: avoid Front Ring 1 + Rear Cog 7, or Front Ring 3 + Rear Cog 1.
Brake gently and progressively — sudden braking on gravel or wet roads causes skids.
Check your tire pressure before every ride. MTB tires should be 30–50 PSI.
Eat a banana or small meal 30–60 minutes before a ride for sustained energy.
After riding, stretch your quads, calves, and hamstrings for at least 5 minutes.
Practice emergency braking in a safe area — brake 70% rear, 30% front on dry roads.
Coconut water is a natural electrolyte drink — perfect and affordable in the Philippines.
Ride with a buddy until you're confident. Safety in numbers, especially on busy roads.
Always signal your turns using hand signals — left arm out for left, right arm out for right.
Look ahead 10–15 meters, not just the road immediately in front of you.
Use your middle chainring (Ring 2) for most flat road riding — it's the most efficient.
Invest in padded cycling shorts early — your sit bones will thank you after long rides.
Sun exposure is intense in the Philippines — apply SPF 50+ sunscreen on exposed skin.
Keep your elbows slightly bent when riding — rigid arms transfer every bump to your back.
A well-fitted bike is the single biggest comfort upgrade. Visit a local bike shop for fitting.
Interval training — alternating fast and slow pedaling — builds speed faster than steady rides.
Hill climbing tip: stay seated, keep cadence high, don't stand until you absolutely have to.
Your saddle height should let your leg be almost fully extended at the bottom of the pedal stroke.
Regular riders get sick less often — cycling boosts immune function over time.
Rest days are part of training. Your muscles grow during recovery, not during the ride.
Bring a small repair kit every ride: inner tube, tire levers, mini pump, multi-tool.
In Tagaytay climbs, shift to your lowest gear combo (Ring 1 + Cog 1–3) and spin, don't grind.
Cycling burns 400–600 calories per hour — great for weight management without joint stress.
Start with 30-minute rides 3x per week, then add 10% distance each week. Consistency beats intensity.
Riding in the rain? Reduce tire pressure by 5 PSI for better grip on wet roads.
Track your rides even if it's just noting the distance. Progress is motivating.
Replace your bike chain every 2,000–3,000 km — a worn chain destroys cassettes and chainrings.
Zone 2 cardio (conversational pace) for 60–90 min builds your aerobic base fastest.
Eat within 30 minutes post-ride: protein + carbs. Eggs + rice or tuna sandwich are great PH options.
Your first gear (Ring 1, Cog 1) is your bailout gear — never be ashamed to use it on brutal climbs.
Group rides in Facebook biking communities are a great way to learn routes and get motivation.
Cycling improves mental health — 30 minutes of riding can reduce stress hormones significantly.
Every Filipino cyclist's bucket list: BGC loop, Tagaytay windmills, La Trinidad, Bataan Death March road.
Wear bright colors or a reflective vest when riding at dawn, dusk, or night.
Check your brakes by squeezing them hard before every ride. They should feel firm, not spongy.
Never draft directly behind a vehicle on the road — ride 1–2 meters to the side for visibility.
Downhill riding: feather the brakes (light constant pressure), don't grab hard and release.
Core strength is as important as leg strength — planks and sit-ups improve cycling posture.
Smiling while riding is not silly — it relaxes your face, jaw, and shoulders, reducing fatigue.
Wipe down your bike after every ride — Filipino roads and humidity accelerate rust and wear.
Gears 2-4 in the rear (middle range) are your sweet spot for flat roads at moderate speed.
A chamois cream on long rides prevents saddle sores — a problem no one talks about but everyone faces.
Set a specific goal: finish a 50km route, do the Tagaytay climb, or ride 30 consecutive days.
Every hill you conquer is proof that yesterday's hard ride made you stronger today.
